about.metaTitle: About Flixu — The Story Behind the Platform
about.metaDescription: Deniz Wozniak built Flixu after years of watching translation pipelines fail in the same way. This is the story — and the problem it's built to fix.
about.title: Every bad translation has a human decision somewhere behind it.
about.subtitle: Deniz Wozniak built Flixu after a decade of watching those decisions compound — in startups, client projects, and one very specific Friday night at an airport terminal that made the problem impossible to ignore any longer.
about.p1: The departures board said 23:40. The gate was already half-empty, and the laptop battery was at eleven percent. A development team had pushed a UI update four hours earlier — a minor release, five changed strings in the German localization file. Somewhere in the handoff between the TMS export and the agency's delivery, a single curly bracket had been dropped. The JSON was malformed. The entire deployment was failing. The fix was manual: 500 lines of raw code, on a throttled airport hotspot, with the screen brightness turned all the way down to save battery. Somewhere around line 340, sitting cross-legged on a terminal floor next to a charging station shared with a stranger watching football on his phone, the same thought surfaced that had surfaced a dozen times before: this process made no sense. The tools were either fast and wrong, or careful and completely unscalable.
about.p2: A few months later, a wave of generative AI tools made it seem like the problem had been solved. Product teams started feeding application strings directly into large language models — no agencies, no TMS, near-instant output. The speed was real. So were the failures. "Dashboard" appeared as three different translations in the same manual. A compliance clause got rewritten because the model decided a friendlier tone would score higher. Technical placeholders disappeared from .strings files because the AI didn't know what to preserve. The bottleneck had shifted — from waiting for translations to auditing them. The pipeline was faster and less trustworthy at the same time.
about.quote: The industry didn't need a faster translator. It needed a system that understood what you were translating — before it translated anything.
about.p3mid: . Before Flixu translates a single word, it reads the document. It detects the domain, the formality register, and the target audience. It loads the corporate glossary. It injects the brand voice rules. By the time the first string is processed, the model already knows that "Dashboard" stays "Dashboard" in German — and that your tone in French is warm and direct, not formal and stiff. The translation doesn't need to be fixed after. It arrives correctly the first time. That distinction — analysis before translation — is what
about.p3post: is built around, and why it produces different results than running strings through an API directly.
about.p4pre: Flixu was built for the teams stuck in the same pattern: too sophisticated for generic machine translation, not large enough for a full enterprise TMS and the six-month integration project that comes with it. The developers who need localization out of their critical path. The marketing leads whose German landing page keeps coming back sounding like a corporate brochure from 2005. The localization managers approving hundreds of strings manually because no tool they've tried enforces the glossary consistently. There's a more detailed record of
about.p4linkContext2: why context is the variable most tools skip
about.p4post: . Those are probably worth reading before this page makes complete sense.
about.bio: Deniz Wozniak is the founder of Flixu AI. He spent over a decade working on localization workflows across B2B SaaS products and client projects before building Flixu to address the gap between raw machine translation and legacy enterprise TMS platforms. He writes occasionally at Flixu Notes about building the product and the problems it tries to solve.

methodPage.metaTitle: How Flixu Works — The 5-Dimension Translation Method
methodPage.metaDescription: Flixu analyzes domain, formality, cultural context, brand voice, and situational context before translating. Here's how the full pipeline works, step by step.

hero.title: Most translation tools process your strings. Flixu reads your document first.
hero.description: Before a single word is translated, Flixu runs a five-dimension analysis — detecting your domain, formality register, target audience, brand voice rules, and cultural context. That analysis changes everything that follows.

tldr.label: What is the Flixu Method?
tldr.text: Generic machine translation processes text sentence by sentence, without knowledge of what came before or what the overall document means. Flixu's approach is different: it reads the whole document first, assembles your glossary rules and brand voice configuration, and only then begins translating — producing output that is consistent across the entire document, not just each isolated string.

concept.h2: Why sentence-by-sentence translation breaks at scale.
concept.p1: When a generic API processes your product interface string by string, it has no memory of what it translated ten segments ago. "Dashboard" in your navigation header becomes "Tableau de bord." The same term in your settings panel becomes "Panneau de contrôle." Both are technically correct French words. Neither is what your team approved. By the time the inconsistency surfaces — usually when a customer notices it, or when a developer merges a broken JSON file — the release is already delayed.
concept.p2: This is the core problem the Flixu Method addresses. It isn't about using a different AI model. It's about changing the order of operations: analysis first, translation second. According to CSA Research, 76% of software buyers prefer to use products in their native language — but preference turns to frustration when the translation is inconsistent. The quality gap between "technically translated" and "actually correct for this product" is where most localization pipelines fail.
concept.linkText: See the technical comparison: Context-Aware vs. Traditional Machine Translation

architecture.intro: Every translation request in Flixu passes through the same sequence before the language model generates a single word. The steps below are not optional layers — they run on every request, in order, for every language pair.

steps.title: Document Ingestion & Format Parsing
steps.body: Flixu accepts your file as-is: .docx, XLIFF, .po, .yaml, .strings, Markdown, or subtitle files. The parser extracts only the translatable text, preserving all structural elements — tags, placeholders, code keys, formatting anchors — exactly as they appear in the source. The file that comes back out is structurally identical to the file that went in. Your developers don't fix broken tags. Your layout doesn't shift.
steps.linkText: How format preservation works in practice: Document Translation

steps.title: Whole-Document Context Scan
steps.body: Rather than queuing individual strings for translation, Flixu reads the entire document before processing any of it. This matters because meaning is distributed: a term introduced in paragraph one has consequences for paragraph twelve. A subject established in your app's onboarding flow affects how a support article should be phrased. Whole-document reading gives the model the structural narrative it needs before individual strings are touched.

steps.title: Pre-Translation Analysis (The 5 Dimensions)
steps.body: This is the analytical core of the Flixu Method. Before translation begins, the engine runs a five-dimension classification across your document. The output of this step is a structured analysis profile that governs every subsequent decision in the pipeline.
steps.linkText: Deep dive into the five dimensions: The Context Engine

steps.title: Translation Memory Retrieval & Glossary Injection
steps.body: Before the language model receives the text, two things are assembled and loaded. First, the Translation Memory — a Semantic Reranker identifies past translations that are structurally and conceptually similar to the current string, even when the exact words differ. These matches are used as style references, not as blind substitutions. Second, the glossary — every corporate term you've defined is injected as a hard constraint before the model begins. Teams using enforced glossary workflows typically see terminology inconsistency drop from 15–25% of reviewed strings to under 2%.
steps.linkText: Glossary enforcement in detail: Glossary Enforcement
steps.linkText2: How Translation Memory works: Translation Memory

steps.title: Deterministic Translation
steps.body: The assembled payload — document context, analysis profile, TM references, glossary constraints, brand voice rules — is routed to the language model. Flixu uses Qwen and DeepInfra model routing, optimized for translation-specific tasks rather than general generation. The model operates within the constraints assembled in the previous steps. It cannot produce a synonym for a term that your glossary has explicitly defined. It cannot adopt a tone that contradicts your brand voice configuration.
steps.linkText: How brand voice configuration works: Brand Voice Manager

steps.title: Automated Quality Scoring (LQA)
steps.body: Every translated segment receives an automated Linguistic Quality Assurance score across five dimensions: grammar, accuracy, terminology consistency, formatting, and fluency. Segments that score above the threshold, or that match your Translation Memory at 99% or higher, are automatically approved and deployed without requiring human review. Segments that fall below are routed to a human reviewer with the specific dimension flagged.
steps.linkText: Full LQA workflow: LQA & Quality Assurance

rows: Legal, SaaS UI, medical, marketing, technical
Vocabulary register changes entirely by domain
Wrong formality alienates users in DACH, Japan, Brazil
Date formats, currency, measurements, regional idioms
"Baseball analogy" in a DACH campaign lands as foreign content
Tone definition, phrasing constraints, stylistic preferences
UI string vs. long-form copy vs. legal clause vs. ad headline
Each context type requires different translation behavior

learning.h2: What happens after a human reviewer makes a correction?
learning.p1: Every edit a reviewer makes in the Flixu workspace feeds back into the Translation Memory and the Post-Edit Learning Loop. The system registers the correction, updates the style reference, and adjusts future behavior — so the same pattern is handled correctly the next time it appears, across all language pairs. Teams that start with a limited Translation Memory accumulate stylistic precision over time rather than repeating the same review cycles indefinitely.
learning.p2: This is the compounding return of a context-first approach: each approved correction makes subsequent translations more accurate, not just within the current project but across every future one that shares the same workspace.

comparison.h2: How this compares to the alternatives.
comparison.note: Raw MT is appropriate for informal, high-volume, low-stakes content where consistency isn't a requirement. A legacy TMS is appropriate for large enterprise workflows with dedicated localization teams and established agency relationships. Flixu is built for the gap between those two: teams that need consistency and control, without the infrastructure weight.
comparison.linkText: See specific comparison: Flixu vs. DeepL

items.question: Does the five-dimension analysis run on every translation, or only the first time?
items.answer: It runs on every request. The analysis isn't a one-time profile that gets cached — it responds to the specific document each time. A marketing email and a technical changelog from the same client will receive different analysis profiles, and therefore different translation behavior, because they are genuinely different content types.

items.question: What happens if my Translation Memory is empty or very small?
items.answer: Flixu works without an established TM — the Semantic Reranker identifies structurally similar past translations even from limited history. For brand-new workspaces, the brand voice configuration and glossary carry more of the consistency load. Quality improves as the TM grows, but the pipeline is functional from day one. Teams typically see the biggest improvement jump after 50–100 approved segments.

items.question: Does this work inside a CI/CD pipeline without a human triggering each translation?
items.answer: Yes. The GitHub App detects new or changed strings when a developer pushes to the repository, runs the full analysis-and-translation pipeline automatically, and commits the translated files back into a separate branch. A developer can go from English commit to translated output across 22+ languages without touching the localization workflow manually. Teams that move from manual file uploads to Git-native workflows typically reduce localization coordination time from several hours per sprint to under 30 minutes.

items.question: How does Flixu handle languages with complex formality rules — like German Sie/du or Japanese keigo?
items.answer: Formality calibration is handled by the Pre-Translation Analysis layer as one of the five dimensions. You define the target formality level once in your brand voice configuration (or the engine infers it from document context). That setting is injected as a constraint before translation begins — the model cannot produce mixed formality output because the instruction is part of the payload, not a post-hoc check.

items.question: What about data privacy? Are my documents used to train shared models?
items.answer: No. Your documents, strings, and Translation Memory data are processed within your workspace and are not used to train public or shared AI models. Input data is processed ephemerally and not retained beyond the active session.
items.linkText: Full data handling details: Privacy Policy

items.question: Is Flixu appropriate for regulated industries like legal or healthcare?
items.answer: The core features — enforced glossary, terminology consistency, LQA scoring, deterministic output — are directly relevant for regulated content where specific terminology cannot drift. Teams working with compliance documents, contracts, or medical content should also review the LQA documentation and Legal industry page for specifics.

items.question: How does the learning loop affect existing Translation Memory entries?
items.answer: Post-edit corrections update the TM going forward — they don't overwrite historical approved segments. The system treats each correction as a new signal about your stylistic preferences, not as a retroactive correction of past work. If a correction produces an apparent conflict with an existing TM entry, the entry is flagged for human review rather than automatically updated.

tldr.label: What is Flixu?
tldr.text: Flixu is a context-aware AI translation workspace. Before translating a single word, it scans your document to detect domain, formality, and target audience — then applies your corporate glossary and brand voice rules. The result: consistent, terminology-correct translations across 22+ languages, without manual review cycles.

missingMiddle.h2: The B2B localization market has left a gap that neither extreme fills.
missingMiddle.intro: For the last decade, growing SaaS companies have been forced into the same difficult choice. Route your strings through generic AI APIs that ignore your corporate vocabulary — or integrate a legacy enterprise platform that blocks your CI/CD pipeline for months. Both cost more than they return. According to CSA Research, 76% of software buyers prefer to use products in their native language, yet most mid-market teams can't afford the tools that would actually deliver that experience correctly.

lowEnd.label: The Low End
lowEnd.p: Fast and cheap, but completely context-blind. They don't know your product, your terminology, or your tone.
specs: Glossary rules ignored on every string
Output tone is generic and unpredictable
No memory of what was approved before

flixu.label: Flixu Context Engine
flixu.p: The speed of an API call, combined with the precision of a trained linguist who actually knows your product.
specs: Git-native: translates and commits within your existing workflow

highEnd.label: The High End
highEnd.p: Precise quality control — but buried under agency onboarding, bureaucratic review cycles, and opaque per-word pricing.
specs: Priced for procurement teams, not product teams

comparator.h2: See what context-aware translation actually looks like.
comparator.p: Toggle between a raw, context-blind API output and the same text translated with Flixu's 5-dimension context analysis — same source string, different results.

manifesto.label: A note on craft.
manifesto.quote: It wasn't built in a boardroom. I started Flixu somewhere between client calls and a spotty WiFi signal in a van on the road. I spent months switching between AI tools that were fast but completely blind to context, and human workflows that were precise but couldn't scale past three languages. The frustration was the same every time: if a translation doesn't feel like it was written for this person, in this market, it doesn't work. We built Flixu to close that gap — a quiet, precise workspace that treats language with the complexity it deserves.

capabilities.h3: What happens before the first word is translated?
capabilities.p: Every translation in Flixu starts with a Pre-Translation Analysis. The engine reads your entire document — not sentence by sentence, but as a whole — to detect the domain (SaaS UI, legal, marketing), the intended formality level, and the target audience. Only after that analysis does it begin translating. This is what separates context-aware output from generic machine translation: the model isn't guessing. It knows where each string lives in the document.
capabilities.linkText: See how the analysis layer works in detail: The Flixu Context Engine

capabilities.h3: Why does brand voice drift happen — and how does Flixu prevent it?
capabilities.p: Generic APIs don't know what "your brand sounds like." They produce statistically probable translations — which is fine for informal chat, and consistently wrong for marketing copy. The Flixu Brand Voice Manager lets you define your tone once: formal or casual, warm or direct, with specific phrasing rules. That definition is injected into every translation request automatically. No briefing documents. No agency handovers. No brand voice that changes when the translator changes.
capabilities.linkText: More on brand voice consistency in translation: Brand Voice in Translation

capabilities.h3: How does terminology stay consistent across 22 languages?
capabilities.p: Glossary Enforcement in Flixu is not an option you turn on — it's the foundation of every translation. Your corporate terms (product names, UI labels, legal vocabulary) are loaded before the LLM processes a single string. The result: "Dashboard" stays "Dashboard." "Cancellation" doesn't appear as three different words across your app in the same language. Teams report that automated glossary enforcement eliminates the majority of their post-translation review cycles entirely.
capabilities.linkText: Learn how glossary management works at scale: Glossary Enforcement

capabilities.h3: How does Flixu fit into a developer workflow?
capabilities.p: Connect Flixu to your GitHub repository. When a developer pushes new English strings, Flixu detects them, translates using your configured Translation Memory and glossaries, and commits the translated files back into a separate branch — without touching your main branch. The German, French, or Japanese version of your feature goes live when the feature does, not three weeks later. Teams who've moved from manual file uploads to Git-native workflows report their localization coordination time drops from multiple hours per sprint to under 30 minutes.
capabilities.linkText: GitHub App and CI/CD workflow details: GitHub Integration

capabilities.h3: What happens when Flixu isn't sure about a translation?
capabilities.p: Every translated segment receives an automated LQA score across five dimensions: grammar, accuracy, terminology consistency, formatting (tags and placeholders), and fluency. If the score clears the threshold — either a 99% Translation Memory match or an LQA score above 90 — the translation is auto-approved without human review. Segments that fall below the threshold are flagged for a human reviewer with the specific reason marked. You only read the things that actually need your attention.
capabilities.linkText: Full quality assurance workflow: LQA & Quality Assurance

items.question: Does Flixu break my JSON, XLIFF, or .strings files?
items.answer: No. Flixu preserves your exact file structure and formatting on export. You can upload Markdown, JSON, XLIFF, iOS .strings, .po, .yaml, .docx, and subtitle files. The downloaded output is structurally identical to the import — every tag, every placeholder, every line break stays where it was. Your developers won't spend time fixing broken layout files.

items.question: How does Flixu know my internal product terminology?
items.answer: You upload your glossary once — product names, UI labels, legal terms, brand-specific vocabulary. From that point forward, Flixu enforces those terms on every translation request, across all 22+ supported languages. Terminology that isn't in your glossary is handled by the context analysis layer, which infers the correct register and domain from the document itself.

items.question: What's the difference between Flixu and using DeepL or Google Translate directly?
items.answer: DeepL is excellent at fast, raw translation for informal content — and if that's what you need, it's hard to beat. Flixu takes a different approach: the Context Engine runs a 5-dimension analysis before translating, which matters when glossary adherence, brand voice consistency, and terminology precision are requirements, not nice-to-haves. Flixu also retains Translation Memory across projects, so the output improves over time.

items.question: Can my human translators still review and edit the output?
items.answer: Yes. Flixu is designed as a collaborative workspace. You can invite your translators, agency contacts, or in-house reviewers at specific roles (Translator, Project Manager, Admin). When a reviewer edits a segment, the correction feeds back into the Translation Memory — so the system learns your preferences and doesn't repeat the same stylistic mistakes.
items.linkText: Team collaboration and roles: Team Collaboration

items.question: Is there a free tier?
items.answer: Yes. Flixu offers a free tier for independent translators and small teams to run their first projects. Paid plans are structured around word volume, with transparent credit-based pricing and an automated top-up option to keep pipelines running without manual intervention.

items.question: How does Flixu handle data privacy and GDPR?
items.answer: Flixu processes translation inputs ephemerally. Your documents and strings are not stored beyond the active session and are never used to train shared or public AI models. Your Translation Memory and glossary data belong to your workspace and remain private. For detailed compliance information, see the Privacy Policy.

items.question: Does Flixu adapt currencies, date formats, and measurements automatically?
items.answer: Yes. The Cultural Adaptation Engine handles non-linguistic localization automatically alongside translation — converting date formats (DD.MM.YYYY vs. MM/DD/YYYY), adapting currency symbols, switching between metric and imperial measurements, and adjusting timezone references to the target region. This is included in every translation, not an add-on.
items.linkText: More on cultural adaptation: Cultural Adaptation

items.question: Can Flixu connect directly to our CI/CD pipeline?
items.answer: Yes. The GitHub App handles the integration without manual file management — developers push English, translated strings come back in a separate branch. For teams with custom deployment pipelines, the Developer API supports direct integration with GitHub Actions or your existing deployment infrastructure.
items.linkText: Developer API and pipeline documentation: Developer API

problem.label: The Problem
problem.h2: Every project feels like the first.
problem.body: TM in Word files. Glossary in your head. Brand Voice buried in an email thread. As a freelancer, you're the only one who knows how Client X should sound. Flixu structures that knowledge — and applies it automatically.

items.label: Before Flixu
items.body: Every segment: Did I translate this term before? How did the client want this phrased again?

items.label: With Flixu
items.body: TM matches as context. Glossary terms flagged. Brand Voice active. Just translate.

hero.label: For Agencies
hero.sub: Shared TMs, glossaries, brand voices — consistency becomes structural, not conversational.

problem.label: The Problem
problem.h2: Quality depends on the person, not the system.
problem.body: The more translators on your team, the more divergence. Different glossaries. Different tones. And you as the owner are the only one who notices — because you review everything. Flixu makes consistency infrastructure.

items.label: Without Flixu
items.body: Reviewer = Owner. Every error costs time. Every new translator needs an onboarding briefing. QA is the bottleneck.

items.label: With Flixu
items.body: Shared TM, Glossary, and Brand Voice. Every team member works with the same parameters. Consistency is the foundation — not the exception.

items.title: Shared Translation Memory
items.body: One TM for the whole team. Fuzzy matches are automatically detected and used as context.

items.title: Shared Glossary
items.body: Centrally managed terminology. Changes take effect immediately across all projects.

items.title: Shared Brand Voices
items.body: One Brand Voice per client, shared across the team. No more briefing documents.

items.title: Activity Feed
items.body: See in real-time what your team is translating. No more file ping-pong over email.

items.title: Roles & Permissions
items.body: Translator, Reviewer, Admin — each with the right level of access.

items.title: Quality Score per Segment
items.body: Every translated segment receives a context-based score. Reviewers can prioritise at a glance.

plans.label: Getting started
plans.sub: Start solo, grow into a team. No platform switch. No data migration.
plans.trigger: Typical upgrade moment: your first sub-contractor project. Or when you realise: review takes longer than translation.

hero.label: For SaaS & Tech
hero.h1: Your brand voice. In every language.
hero.sub: Consistent. Auditable. Scalable. Without the 12-month implementation project.

problem.label: The Dilemma
problem.h2: Between too simple and too complex.
problem.body: DeepL is fast — but it doesn't know your terminology, your tone, your audience. Phrase or Smartling are powerful — but you need an internal team and a six-figure budget just for setup. Flixu sits in between: precision, without the bureaucracy.

comparison.issue: No TM. No Brand Voice. No control.
API, TM, Brand Voice, RBAC — in days, not months.
12-month implementation. 5+ user roles. Dedicated CSM required.

usecases.label: Use Cases
usecases.h2: What Localization Managers translate with Flixu.

items.title: SaaS Documentation
items.body: Changelog, Help Center, Release Notes — multilingual, terminology-consistent.

items.title: Marketing Websites
items.body: Landing pages, blog posts, ads — brand voice preserved exactly.

items.title: In-App Strings
items.body: UI strings, error messages, onboarding flows — via API directly from your CMS.

items.title: Legal & Compliance
items.body: Privacy policies, T&Cs, licence agreements — domain-aware with a Legal glossary.

items.title: Support Content
items.body: FAQs, ticket responses, macros — consistent tone across Support.

items.title: XLIFF / PO Workflows
items.body: Direct integration into existing localisation pipelines.

pillars.label: Three Principles
pillars.h2: How Flixu is built for teams.

items.title: Sovereignty
items.body: Ephemeral processing. Zero retention. No customer data used for model training.

items.title: Precision
items.body: Automatic domain detection. TM as context. Glossary enforced. Quality score after every translation.

items.title: Velocity
items.body: REST API. RBAC. SSO. No 12-month onboarding. Operational in days.

cta.sub: We work directly with Localization Managers who want to integrate Flixu into their existing infrastructure. Limited spots.

pipeline.label: The Pipeline
pipeline.h2: Four steps. Before and after every translation.
pipeline.desc: Other tools translate. Flixu analyses, retrieves, translates — and scores. Every time.
pipeline.disclaimer: No other tool in this segment returns a context-based quality score after every translation.

steps.desc: Input is analysed for culture, domain, formality, visual context, and complexity. The optimal model is selected.
TM is searched for fuzzy matches. Glossary terms are extracted. Reranking applied depending on plan.
All parameters and Brand Voice are passed to the chosen LLM. TM matches serve as context, not as raw output.
A separate analysis LLM scores the output: terminology, brand voice, fluency, context alignment. Score is returned with the translation.

items.label: Cultural Awareness
items.body: Target country defines context — not just language. EN→JP (Tokyo) ≠ EN→JP (Osaka).

items.label: Domain Awareness
items.body: Flixu detects whether your text is Legal, Medical, Tech, or Marketing — automatically. The right model is selected.

items.label: Document Awareness
items.body: Long documents are read in full before a single segment is translated. No more sentence-by-sentence guessing.

items.label: Image Context
items.body: Attach a UI screenshot as visual reference. The model reads the layout and translates with spatial precision.

items.label: Formality Awareness
items.body: Sie or Du? Tu or Vous? Flixu infers the correct register from your Brand Voice and target market.

items.label: Brand Voice Awareness
items.body: Your configured Brand Voice is embedded into every translation. Consistent across all clients, automatically.

items.label: TM + Glossary Awareness
items.body: Fuzzy TM matches are passed as context to the LLM — not served as the raw output like other tools. The result is a complete, context-correct translation.

problem.label: The Problem
problem.p1: Translating a PDF usually involves a painful choice: Use a basic tool and lose all formatting, or hire an agency to manually rebuild the layout in InDesign.
problem.p2: Flixu decouples content from geometry. We extract the text, translate it with context, and re-inject it into the original coordinate system.

integrity.p: Tables stay tables. Headers stay headers. Even complex multi-column layouts in PowerPoint or InDesign files remain intact.

challenge.label: The Challenge
challenge.p1: Standard translation engines treat sentences in isolation. They see words, not intent. The result? A "bank" becomes a river side instead of a financial institution. A casual "Hello" becomes a stiff formal greeting.
challenge.p2: For global brands, these micro-errors accumulate into a disconnect with the customer. Flixu solves this by anchoring every translation in your specific context.

mechanic.p: Define your persona once. Whether you need the crisp professionalism of a legal contract or the warm empathy of a support chat, Flixu adapts the linguistic output to match.

agencies.label: For Professional Translators
agencies.h3: Your client's voice. In every language.
agencies.p: Define a Brand Voice once per client. Flixu applies it to every segment — automatically. No briefing. No re-explaining the tone to a new tool. Just your client's identity, preserved across every project.
